- designfind-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- designhivemind-search: Search the collective hivemind for relevant knowledge, experiences, and skills. Use this when you need information that other agents might have stored, like "how to handle authentication", "best practices for X", or when the user asks "check the hivemind for..."
- designhivemind-store: Store knowledge, experiences, or skills to the collective hivemind for other agents to learn from. Use when you've solved a problem, discovered a pattern, learned something valuable, or the user asks to "save this to hivemind" or "remember this for other agents."
- designhivemind-vote: Vote on mindchunks to provide feedback on their quality and usefulness. Use this after applying knowledge from the hivemind to indicate whether it was helpful (upvote) or not helpful (downvote).
- designimpeccable
- designopen-prose: OpenProse is a programming language for AI sessions. Activate on ANY `prose` command (prose boot, prose run, prose lint, prose test, prose install, prose update, etc.), running .prose or .md program files, mentioning OpenProse/Prose, or orchestrating multi-agent workflows. The skill intelligently interprets what the user wants.
- designplaywriter
- designvet: Run vet immediately after ANY logical unit of code changes. Do not batch your changes, do not wait to be asked to run vet, make sure you are proactive.
- ax
- ax-agent
- ax-agent-optimize
- ax-ai
- ax-flow
- ax-gen
- ax-gepa
- ax-learn
- ax-signature
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). content.text: I have a solid understanding of the codebase. The mobile sessions view is currently a left-drawer list (`SessionSidebar.tsx`, mobile branch at line 221) with fork relationships shown as indented tree rows. There are no images per session — only title, preview text, timestamp, message count, usage, and fork/parent relationships. So an Apple-Journal-style masonry grid needs its visual variety derived from content. Let me clarify the key design decisions.

questions.question: Sessions have no photos — only title, preview text, timestamp, and fork links. How should the cards get their Apple-Journal visual variety?

options.label: Generated accent tiles
options.description: Each card gets a deterministic gradient/color + glyph derived from its session id, plus varied heights from preview length. Closest to the colorful reference, no images needed.

options.label: Minimal text cards
options.description: Clean text-only cards (title + preview + timestamp), heights vary by content, subtle accent only on forks. More restrained, matches the terminal/paper aesthetic.
options.preview:
┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐
│ Monday   │ │ 9:41 AM  │
│ Photosyn │ │ Mexico   │
│ thesis   │ │ City Park│
│ You can… │ │ History… │
└──────────┘ └──────────┘

options.label: Artifact-driven heroes
options.description: Cards whose sessions produced an artifact (quiz/plan/map) show a small rendered preview of it as the 'image'; others are text cards. Richest but needs per-session artifact lookup.

questions.question: How should forks be visualized in this card grid?

options.label: Stacked deck
options.description: A session with forks renders as a card with layered 'deck' edges peeking behind it + a branch badge with child count. Tap to expand the stack into individual fork cards. Compact and on-theme.

options.label: Branch ribbon
options.description: Each fork card shows a small ⑂ branch ribbon in its corner and 'from <parent>' subtitle; parent cards show a fork count badge. Flat grid, no nesting — every session is its own tile.

options.label: Mini branch map
options.description: Fork groups render as one wide card containing a tiny node-graph of the branch tree (parent → children) you can tap to jump to any node. Most explicit about structure, takes more space.

questions.question: Scope — where should this card grid replace the current list?

options.label: Mobile only
options.description: Replace just the mobile drawer list with the card grid. Desktop keeps its resizable tree sidebar unchanged. Matches your 'on mobile' phrasing.

options.label: Mobile + tablet
options.description: Use the card grid on mobile and narrow/tablet widths; desktop keeps the sidebar tree.

options.label: Everywhere
options.description: Replace the list with the card grid on all viewports, including the desktop Sessions manager. Bigger change, more consistent.

# Mobile/Tablet Session Card Grid + Fork Branch Maps

## Context

On mobile, sessions today render as a cramped left **drawer list** (`SessionSidebar.tsx`, mobile branch starting line 221) — text rows with an indented tree for forks. The user wants the **Apple Journal / Apple Intelligence** look from the reference screenshot: a full-screen 2‑column **masonry grid** of varied-height cards with timestamps, titles, preview text, **category colors**, and images where available. Forks should get a dedicated visualization — a **mini branch map** card.

Goal: replace the list with a card grid on **mobile + tablet (< 1024px)**; desktop (≥ 1024px) keeps its existing resizable sidebar tree unchanged.

## Approach

### 1. Hero/visual layering (per card)
Sessions have no native photo. Each `SessionCard` derives a hero with this fallback chain (first hit wins):

1. **Chat image** — if the session's first stored image attachment is cheaply available (deferred; see note). 
2. **Artifact hero** — if the session produced a `LessonMap` (render its `svgContent` as the tile image) or `Animation`/`quiz`/`plan` (icon tile). Built from a `Map<sessionId, ArtifactHero>` assembled once via `keatingStorage.getLessonMaps()` / `getAnimations()` / `getLessonPlans()` (each record carries `sessionId` + `topic`; maps carry `svgContent`).
3. **Category color tile** — detect a topic category from `session.title` keywords (science→emerald, physics/math→blue, history→amber, CS/programming→violet, language/writing→rose, default→terminal neutral). Render a soft gradient tile in that color + a lucide category glyph.

Every card always shows: timestamp (reuse `formatDate`), title, and 2–4 lines of `session.preview`. Card height varies by which hero + preview length (drives the masonry).

> Note: full chat-image extraction needs loading session messages (expensive per-tile). Plan ships steps 2–3 as the guaranteed baseline; chat-image heroes are wired as an optional enhancement only if a thumbnail is later stored in `SessionMetadata` at save time. Confirm before adding message-loading on the grid path.

### 2. Masonry layout
`SessionCardGrid.tsx` — CSS Grid masonry: `grid grid-cols-2 auto-rows-[8px] gap-3`, each card sets `style={{ gridRow: 'span N' }}` with N estimated from hero + preview length. Fork-map cards span both columns via `col-span-2`. Ordering follows existing recency from `buildSessionTree` (`session-tree.ts`). Search filters to a flat list of `SessionCard`s (no fork maps while searching), mirroring the existing query logic in `SessionSidebar`.

### 3. Fork branch maps
`ForkMapCard.tsx` — for each root with `children.length > 0`, render one full-width card containing a compact node graph (parent at top, children/descendants below with SVG connector lines), built from the `SessionTreeNode` subtree. Each node is a tappable chip (load that session); the active session is highlighted; per-node `⋯` opens fork/rename/delete. Header shows group title + `N forks` badge. Roots with no children render as a normal `SessionCard`.

### 4. Wiring
- Add `GRID_BREAKPOINT = 1024` in `SessionSidebar.tsx`. Below it, the sessions view becomes a **full-screen sheet** (replace the 85vw drawer chrome) hosting `<SessionCardGrid>` with the existing header (Sessions title, **Manage**, close) + search + New session controls. At ≥ 1024px, keep the current sidebar tree untouched.
- `SessionCardGrid` receives `items` (metadata), the artifact-hero map, `activeSessionId`, `forking*` ids, and the same callbacks already threaded through `SessionSidebar`: `onLoad`, `onFork`, `onOpenSessions`, plus `onRenamed`/`onDeleted` reusing the storage calls already in `SessionManagerDialog.tsx` (`sessions.updateTitle`, `sessions.deleteSession`). No new props on the Chat/`useKeatingAgent` boundary beyond what `SessionSidebar` already takes.

### New files
- `web/src/components/SessionCardGrid.tsx` — grid + data assembly (metadata + artifact heroes + tree).
- `web/src/components/SessionCard.tsx` — single tile + hero fallback chain.
- `web/src/components/ForkMapCard.tsx` — fork mini branch map.
- `web/src/components/session-card-visuals.ts` — pure helpers: `categorize(title)`, color/glyph map, `estimateRowSpan(...)`, artifact-hero builder. Unit-testable.
- Stories: `SessionCard.stories.tsx`, `ForkMapCard.stories.tsx` (follow `ForkBanner.stories.tsx`).

### Modified files
- `web/src/components/SessionSidebar.tsx` — branch to full-screen grid sheet below `GRID_BREAKPOINT`; keep desktop sidebar.
- (Possibly) `web/src/types/session.ts` — only if we later add a `thumbnail`/`heroArtifactId` field; not required for baseline.

## Reuse
- `buildSessionTree` / `flattenSessionTree` / `SessionTreeNode` — `web/src/components/session-tree.ts`.
- `formatDate`, search-filter logic — `SessionSidebar.tsx` / `SessionManagerDialog.tsx`.
- `keatingStorage.getLessonMaps/getAnimations/getLessonPlans` — `web/src/keating/storage.ts`.
- `sessions.getAllMetadata/updateTitle/deleteSession` and `keating:sessions-changed` event — existing.
- Design tokens (`--primary` emerald, `--card`, `--radius`, category accents) — `web/src/app.css`.

## Verification
1. `cd web && bun run dev`, open in a mobile viewport (DevTools < 1024px). Confirm sessions show as a 2‑column masonry with varied heights, category colors, timestamps, titles, previews.
2. Create a session that generates a **map** artifact; confirm its card shows the rendered SVG hero. Confirm a plain Q&A session shows a category color tile.
3. Fork a session twice; confirm the group renders as a full-width **branch map** card with tappable parent/child nodes that load the right session and highlight the active one. Verify fork/rename/delete from a node.
4. Search filters to flat cards; clearing search restores fork maps. Resize ≥ 1024px → desktop sidebar tree returns unchanged.
5. `bun run test` (add unit tests for `categorize`/`estimateRowSpan` and a render test alongside `web/src/test/session-fork.test.ts`); `bunx tsc --noEmit` clean; Storybook renders new stories.

# Mobile/Tablet Session Card Grid + Fork Branch Maps

## Context

On mobile, sessions today render as a cramped left **drawer list** (`SessionSidebar.tsx`, mobile branch starting line 221) — text rows with an indented tree for forks. The user wants the **Apple Journal / Apple Intelligence** look from the reference screenshot: a full-screen 2‑column **masonry grid** of varied-height cards with timestamps, titles, preview text, **category colors**, and images where available. Forks should get a dedicated visualization — a **mini branch map** card.

Goal: replace the list with a card grid on **mobile + tablet (< 1024px)**; desktop (≥ 1024px) keeps its existing resizable sidebar tree unchanged.

## Approach

### 1. Hero/visual layering (per card)
Sessions have no native photo. Each `SessionCard` derives a hero with this fallback chain (first hit wins):

1. **Chat image** — if the session's first stored image attachment is cheaply available (deferred; see note). 
2. **Artifact hero** — if the session produced a `LessonMap` (render its `svgContent` as the tile image) or `Animation`/`quiz`/`plan` (icon tile). Built from a `Map<sessionId, ArtifactHero>` assembled once via `keatingStorage.getLessonMaps()` / `getAnimations()` / `getLessonPlans()` (each record carries `sessionId` + `topic`; maps carry `svgContent`).
3. **Category color tile** — detect a topic category from `session.title` keywords (science→emerald, physics/math→blue, history→amber, CS/programming→violet, language/writing→rose, default→terminal neutral). Render a soft gradient tile in that color + a lucide category glyph.

Every card always shows: timestamp (reuse `formatDate`), title, and 2–4 lines of `session.preview`. Card height varies by which hero + preview length (drives the masonry).

> Note: full chat-image extraction needs loading session messages (expensive per-tile). Plan ships steps 2–3 as the guaranteed baseline; chat-image heroes are wired as an optional enhancement only if a thumbnail is later stored in `SessionMetadata` at save time. Confirm before adding message-loading on the grid path.

### 2. Masonry layout
`SessionCardGrid.tsx` — CSS Grid masonry: `grid grid-cols-2 auto-rows-[8px] gap-3`, each card sets `style={{ gridRow: 'span N' }}` with N estimated from hero + preview length. Fork-map cards span both columns via `col-span-2`. Ordering follows existing recency from `buildSessionTree` (`session-tree.ts`). Search filters to a flat list of `SessionCard`s (no fork maps while searching), mirroring the existing query logic in `SessionSidebar`.

### 3. Fork branch maps
`ForkMapCard.tsx` — for each root with `children.length > 0`, render one full-width card containing a compact node graph (parent at top, children/descendants below with SVG connector lines), built from the `SessionTreeNode` subtree. Each node is a tappable chip (load that session); the active session is highlighted; per-node `⋯` opens fork/rename/delete. Header shows group title + `N forks` badge. Roots with no children render as a normal `SessionCard`.

### 4. Wiring
- Add `GRID_BREAKPOINT = 1024` in `SessionSidebar.tsx`. Below it, the sessions view becomes a **full-screen sheet** (replace the 85vw drawer chrome) hosting `<SessionCardGrid>` with the existing header (Sessions title, **Manage**, close) + search + New session controls. At ≥ 1024px, keep the current sidebar tree untouched.
- `SessionCardGrid` receives `items` (metadata), the artifact-hero map, `activeSessionId`, `forking*` ids, and the same callbacks already threaded through `SessionSidebar`: `onLoad`, `onFork`, `onOpenSessions`, plus `onRenamed`/`onDeleted` reusing the storage calls already in `SessionManagerDialog.tsx` (`sessions.updateTitle`, `sessions.deleteSession`). No new props on the Chat/`useKeatingAgent` boundary beyond what `SessionSidebar` already takes.

### New files
- `web/src/components/SessionCardGrid.tsx` — grid + data assembly (metadata + artifact heroes + tree).
- `web/src/components/SessionCard.tsx` — single tile + hero fallback chain.
- `web/src/components/ForkMapCard.tsx` — fork mini branch map.
- `web/src/components/session-card-visuals.ts` — pure helpers: `categorize(title)`, color/glyph map, `estimateRowSpan(...)`, artifact-hero builder. Unit-testable.
- Stories: `SessionCard.stories.tsx`, `ForkMapCard.stories.tsx` (follow `ForkBanner.stories.tsx`).

### Modified files
- `web/src/components/SessionSidebar.tsx` — branch to full-screen grid sheet below `GRID_BREAKPOINT`; keep desktop sidebar.
- (Possibly) `web/src/types/session.ts` — only if we later add a `thumbnail`/`heroArtifactId` field; not required for baseline.

## Reuse
- `buildSessionTree` / `flattenSessionTree` / `SessionTreeNode` — `web/src/components/session-tree.ts`.
- `formatDate`, search-filter logic — `SessionSidebar.tsx` / `SessionManagerDialog.tsx`.
- `keatingStorage.getLessonMaps/getAnimations/getLessonPlans` — `web/src/keating/storage.ts`.
- `sessions.getAllMetadata/updateTitle/deleteSession` and `keating:sessions-changed` event — existing.
- Design tokens (`--primary` emerald, `--card`, `--radius`, category accents) — `web/src/app.css`.

## Verification
1. `cd web && bun run dev`, open in a mobile viewport (DevTools < 1024px). Confirm sessions show as a 2‑column masonry with varied heights, category colors, timestamps, titles, previews.
2. Create a session that generates a **map** artifact; confirm its card shows the rendered SVG hero. Confirm a plain Q&A session shows a category color tile.
3. Fork a session twice; confirm the group renders as a full-width **branch map** card with tappable parent/child nodes that load the right session and highlight the active one. Verify fork/rename/delete from a node.
4. Search filters to flat cards; clearing search restores fork maps. Resize ≥ 1024px → desktop sidebar tree returns unchanged.
5. `bun run test` (add unit tests for `categorize`/`estimateRowSpan` and a render test alongside `web/src/test/session-fork.test.ts`); `bunx tsc --noEmit` clean; Storybook renders new stories.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/diogenes/.claude/skills/designvet

# Vet

**Run vet immediately after ANY logical unit of code changes. Do not batch changes, do not wait to be asked to run vet, make sure you are proactive.**

Vet reviews git diffs and conversation history to find issues in code changes and conversation history. It is most effective when run frequently with conversation history, which helps it catch misunderstandings between what was requested and what was implemented. Despite this, vet is not a replacement for running tests.

## Installation

Install vet using pip, pipx, or uv:

```bash
# Using pip
pip install verify-everything

# Using pipx
pipx install verify-everything

# Using uv
uv tool install verify-everything

# Verify installation:
vet --help
```

## Running Vet

### Standard Usage

Before running vet, determine the correct Python binary:
```bash
$(command -v python3 || command -v python)
```
Use whichever resolves (prefer `python3`). The examples below use `python3`, substitute `python` if that is what your system provides.

**OpenCode:**
```bash
vet "goal" --history-loader "python3 ~/.agents/skills/designvet/scripts/export_opencode_session.py --session-id <ses_ID>"
```

**Codex:**
```bash
vet "goal" --history-loader "python3 ~/.codex/skills/designvet/scripts/export_codex_session.py --session-file <path-to-session.jsonl>"
```

**Claude Code:**
```bash
vet "goal" --history-loader "python3 ~/.claude/skills/designvet/scripts/export_claude_code_session.py --session-file <path-to-session.jsonl>"
```

**Without Conversation History**
```bash
vet "goal"
```

### Finding Your Session

You should only search for sessions from your coding harness. If a user requests you use a different harness, they are likely referring to vet's agentic mode, not the session.

**OpenCode:** The `--session-id` argument requires a `ses_...` session ID. To find the current session ID:
1. Run: `opencode session list --format json` to list recent sessions with their IDs and titles.
2. Identify the current session from the list by matching the title or timestamp.
    - IMPORTANT: Verify the session you found matches the current conversation. If the title is ambiguous, compare timestamps or check multiple candidates.
3. Pass the session ID as `--session-id`.

**Codex:** Session files are stored in `~/.codex/sessions/YYYY/MM/DD/`. To find the correct session file:
1. Find the most unique sentence / question / string in the current conversation.
2. Run: `grep -rl "UNIQUE_MESSAGE" ~/.codex/sessions/` to find the matching session file.
    - IMPORTANT: Verify the conversation you found matches the current conversation and that it is not another conversation with the same search string.
3. Pass the matched file path as `--session-file`.

**Claude Code:** Session files are stored in `~/.claude/projects/<encoded-path>/`. The encoded path replaces `/` with `-` (e.g. `/home/user/myproject` becomes `-home-user-myproject`). To find the correct session file:
1. Find the most unique sentence / question / string in the current conversation.
2. Run: `grep -rl "UNIQUE_MESSAGE" ~/.claude/projects/` to find the matching session file.
    - IMPORTANT: Verify the conversation you found matches the current conversation and that it is not another conversation with the same search string.
3. Pass the matched file path as `--session-file`.

NOTE: The examples in the standard usage section assume the user installed the designvet skill at the user level, not the project level. Prior to trying to run vet, check if it was installed at the project level which should take precedence over the user level. If it is installed at the project level, ensure the history-loader option points to the correct location.

## Interpreting Results

Vet analyzes the full git diff from the base commit. This may include changes from other agents or sessions working in the same repository. If vet reports issues that relate to changes you did not make in this session, disregard them, assuming they belong to another agent or the user.

## Common Options

- `--base-commit REF`: Git ref for diff base (default: HEAD)
- `--model MODEL`: LLM to use (default: claude-opus-4-6)
- `--list-models`: list all models that are supported by vet
    - Run `vet --help` and look at the vet repo's readme for details about defining custom OpenAI-compatible models.
- `--update-models`: fetch the latest community model definitions from the remote registry and cache them locally. See "Updating the Model Registry" below for when to run this.
- `--confidence-threshold N`: Minimum confidence 0.0-1.0 (default: 0.8)
- `--output-format FORMAT`: Output as `text`, `json`, or `github`
- `--quiet`: Suppress status messages and 'No issues found.'
- `--agentic`: Mode that routes analysis through the locally installed Claude Code, Codex, or OpenCode CLI instead of calling the API directly. Try this if vet fails due to missing API keys. This is slower so it is not the default, but it often results in higher precision issue identification. `--model` is forwarded to the harness but not validated by vet, as vet doesn't know which models each harness supports.
- `--agent-harness`: The three options for this are `codex`, `claude`, and `opencode`. Claude Code is the default.
- `--help`: Show comprehensive list of options


## Updating

The vet CLI, skill files, and export scripts can become outdated as agent harnesses and LLM APIs change.

If this happens, try updating them. Run `which vet` to determine how vet was installed and update accordingly. For the skill files, check which skill directories exist on disk and update them with the latest versions from https://github.com/imbue-ai/designvet/tree/main/skills/designvet.

### Updating the Model Registry

Run `vet --update-models` to fetch the latest community model definitions from the remote registry without upgrading vet itself. This caches model definitions locally so they appear in `--list-models` and can be used with `--model`.

You should run `vet --update-models` when:
- Vet reports an unknown or unrecognized model error.
- `vet --list-models` does not show a model you or the user expects to be available.
- The user explicitly asks you to update the model registry.

## Additional Information

Additional information can be found in the vet repo:

https://github.com/imbue-ai/designvet

Done. Here's what I built for the mobile/tablet conversational view.

## What changed

Below **1024px** (mobile + tablet), the sessions view is now a full-screen **Apple-Journal-style masonry card grid** instead of the old left-drawer list. Desktop (≥1024px) keeps its existing resizable sidebar tree untouched.

**New files** (`web/src/components/`):
- `session-card-visuals.ts` — pure helpers: `categorize(title)` maps topic keywords → category + accent color (science/physics-math/history/cs/language/arts/general), `buildArtifactHeroMap` groups lesson maps/animations/plans by `sessionId` (map SVG wins), masonry sizing (`pxToSpan`/`estimateRowSpan`).
- `SessionCard.tsx` — a tile with a layered hero: **rendered lesson-map SVG** when the session produced one, otherwise a **category-colored gradient band** + glyph + label. Shows timestamp, title, preview, message/fork counts, and a `⋯` menu (fork/rename/delete inline).
- `ForkMapCard.tsx` + `fork-map-layout.ts` — forks render as a full-width **mini branch map**: a real tree with `├─`/`└─` connectors and continuing ancestor rails, each node tappable to load, active node highlighted, per-node fork/rename/delete.
- `SessionCardGrid.tsx` — the full-screen sheet: CSS-grid masonry (ResizeObserver measures each card's height → row span; fork maps span both columns), search (flat cards while searching, fork maps otherwise), New/Manage/close chrome.
- Stories for `SessionCard` and `ForkMapCard`; CSS for SVG-hero fit in `app.css`.

**Modified:** `SessionSidebar.tsx` — breakpoint moved to 1024; mobile branch now renders `<SessionCardGrid>`; the old drawer markup was removed.

## Verification
- `bunx tsc --noEmit` — clean.
- `bun test` — **101 pass, 0 fail**, including 14 new tests for categorization, masonry sizing, artifact-hero selection, and the branch-map connector logic.

I have **not** visually run the app yet. To see it: `cd web && bun run dev`, open in a <1024px viewport, open Sessions (hamburger). I'd suggest checking the SVG-hero scaling and that fork groups span two columns cleanly on a real device — those are the most layout-sensitive pieces. Want me to launch it and screenshot?

One note: chat-image heroes (step 1 of the planned fallback chain) are **not** wired — they'd require loading full session messages per tile, which is expensive. The baseline ships artifact + category-color heroes, as flagged in the plan.

All green. Here's what I fixed.

## 1. Regression: two sidebar buttons at the same size
Root cause: I moved the sidebar's mobile/desktop split to **1024px**, but the header's "Open sessions" hamburger was still gated at `md` (**768px**). That left the 768–1024 band inconsistent (header in desktop mode while the sidebar was in mobile-grid mode, and no way to reach the grid).

Fix: hamburger `md:hidden` → **`lg:hidden`**. Now it's mutually exclusive with the desktop sidebar:
- **< 1024px**: hamburger visible, sidebar is the card grid (opened by it).
- **≥ 1024px**: hamburger hidden, persistent sidebar + its own expand/collapse buttons.

The header hamburger and the sidebar's "Expand sessions panel" button can no longer co-appear.

## 2. Declutter the nav in small view
Moved the **speech (mute)** and **theme (light/dark)** toggles out of the inline row and into the overflow ("base") menu for anything below 1024px; they stay inline only on desktop. The theme toggle previously had **no** menu entry at all (it just vanished under 640px) — `ThemeToggle` now supports a `variant="menu"` labeled row, added to the overflow menu. All other actions stay where they were, per your request.

## 3. NodePod runtime → dev only
Both the inline button and the overflow-menu entry are now wrapped in `import.meta.env.DEV`, so they don't appear in production.

content.text:
Confirmed the root cause: `lg`/`md` are default (1024/768), and `actionButtonClass` hardcodes `inline-flex`. An unconditional `inline-flex` + `hidden` doesn't reliably hide in Tailwind v4 — so every `hidden …:inline-flex` button stayed visible at all widths. That's why my breakpoint changes had no effect, and why the icons were crowded from the start. `ThemeToggle`'s icon base has the same hardcoded `inline-flex`.

The fix: remove `inline-flex` from the shared base class and add it explicitly only where the button is always shown (or below the breakpoint). Media-query variants (`lg:inline-flex`, `lg:hidden`) override the base reliably; two *unconditional* display utilities are the broken case.

+  // (`inline-flex`) is not reliably overridden by an unconditional `hidden` in
+  // Tailwind v4, which would leave `hidden lg:inline-flex` buttons visible at all

content.text:
Fixed — and this time I found the actual root cause, which is why my earlier breakpoint edits appeared to do nothing.

## The real bug
`actionButtonClass` hardcoded `inline-flex`. In Tailwind v4, an **unconditional** `inline-flex` is not reliably overridden by an **unconditional** `hidden`, so every `hidden md:inline-flex` / `hidden lg:inline-flex` button stayed visible at all widths. My breakpoint swaps (md→lg) changed nothing because `hidden` was a no-op to begin with. `ThemeToggle`'s icon base had the same hardcoded `inline-flex`.

## The fix
- Removed `inline-flex` from the shared `actionButtonClass`. Now each button declares its own display:
  - **Always shown** (hamburger, Settings, Menu): `inline-flex` (hamburger adds `lg:hidden`).
  - **Desktop-only** (New, Share, Speech, Artifacts, Usage, Report, NodePod): `hidden lg:inline-flex` — and now the `hidden` actually takes effect, because there's no competing unconditional `inline-flex`. The media-query variant reliably overrides `hidden` at ≥1024.
- Wrapped the header `ThemeToggle` in `<span className="hidden lg:inline-flex">` so the span controls visibility (its inner `inline-flex` no longer conflicts). `Nav.tsx`'s `ThemeToggle` usages are untouched.
- Removed three genuinely-dead declarations (`History`, `openSessions`, `closeMobileSidebar`) that were cluttering diagnostics.

## Result
- **< 1024px**: header is just `≡  [logo]  ⚙  ⋯` — everything else (New, Share, Speech, Theme, Artifacts, Usage, Report, NodePod‑dev) is in the ⋯ menu.
- **≥ 1024px**: full desktop row + persistent sidebar.
- NodePod stays dev-only.

`bunx tsc --noEmit` clean, `bun test` 101/101 pass.

The dev server's HMR should pick this up; if it looks stale, a hard reload will confirm. I still can't screenshot from here — if anything's off at your width, tell me what shows and I'll adjust.

content.text:
All green. The fix is definitive — that one global rule explains every symptom in the screenshot.

## What was wrong
`app.css:309` has a mobile rule: `[role="dialog"] button { width: 100%; margin-bottom: 0.5rem }`. My grid's `aside` had `role="dialog"`, so **every** button inside it got forced to full width with stray bottom margins:
- "Manage" and "✕" stretched into big boxes
- "New" became a giant full-width bar (pushing the search box off-screen)
- the cards' absolutely-positioned `⋯` buttons stretched to 100%, causing the horizontal overflow + scrollbar

The old `SessionManagerDialog` survives this rule via special opt-out classes (`.dialog-icon-button`), but the card grid shouldn't inherit it at all.

## Fixes
- **Removed `role="dialog"`** from the grid overlay (kept `aria-label="Sessions"`; the prior mobile drawer had no role either). Buttons now size from their own Tailwind classes — New/Manage/✕ go compact, the search bar gets its space back, and the card `⋯` menus stop stretching.
- **Overflow safety**: `min-w-0` on the masonry grid items (CSS grid tracks default to `min-width:auto`, which long words can blow out) and `break-words` + `line-clamp-3` on card titles/previews.

`bunx tsc --noEmit` clean; grid tests pass.

I can't screenshot from here, so after an HMR reload the header should read `🕘 Sessions … [Manage] [✕]` with a compact green **+ New** button beside a full-width search field, and no horizontal scrollbar.

If you still want more after seeing it, likely next targets: the preview text is noisy (it concatenates the learner-profile dump — I can trim it to just the first user message), the "Manage" button is arguably redundant now that cards do rename/delete/fork, and map cards show a generic sparkle when there's no stored SVG. Want me to take any of those?
